parts.answer: Knowledge 1, Application 2, Analysis 1 Quantitative skills assessed: QS5: Calculate cost, revenue, profit and break-even Knowledge 1 mark for identifying the formula for operating profit: • Gross profit – other operating expenses (1) Application Up to 2 marks for selecting the correct data from Extract B: • $331.3m (1) - $213.1m (1) Analysis 1 mark for calculating the operating profit: • = $118.2m (1) NB: If no working is shown, award marks as follows: • If the answer given is $118.2m or $118 200 000 award 4 • If the answer given is 118.2m or 118 200 000 award 3 (4) Analyse two ways Etsy has reduced the threat of competition in the market. Mark Knowledge Up to 2 marks for the threat of competition, e.g. • The behaviour of competitors (1) that may lead to the loss of market share (1) OR For stating two ways, e.g. • Charging lower prices (1) • Differentiating the products (1) Application Up to 2 marks for answers contextualised to Etsy, e.g. • Sellers only pay a low fee to sell their products via the online shop (1) • Customers can find unique, handmade products on the Etsy website (1) Analysis Up to 2 marks for reasons/causes/consequences for Etsy, e.g. • As customers are usually sensitive to price changes, charging lower prices for handmade products is likely to attract more customers in a competitive market (1) • Having diversity in products offered may enable Etsy to gain market share from its competitors (1) (6) Discuss whether Etsy is likely to be affected by internal causes of business failure. Indicative content

Indicative content guidance Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. Knowledge, Application, Analysis, Evaluation – indicative content • Internal factors are those which a business can control • Business failure can occur due to causes from inside the business, such as poor management of cash flow, overestimating sales, poor marketing, poor quality or overtrading • Etsy may overestimate sales on its website and therefore commit too many resources to advertising or expanding too quickly • This could lead to a decrease in (operating) profit (from $118.2m in the third quarter of 2020) • Poor quality of handmade products is possible due to the high number (3,681) of sellers Etsy allows to sell on its website, because it may not be able to check they all meet a suitable standard • As a result, Etsy may get a bad reputation for allowing those selling poor quality products to trade on its website and the number of buyers may fall from 69 649 in the third quarter of 2020 • Allowing the change for other people to make products aside from the sellers of the original designs could detract from the unique approach that helped Etsy become successful previously • However, Etsy has been trading for over 15 years and is now well-established so overtrading is unlikely to lead to business failure • Etsy has a healthy financial position, with a year-over-year increase in gross profit margin of 8.2% in the third quarter of 2020 and so its management of cash flow may not be a problem for the business • If Etsy increases checks on the products available, it may prevent problems occurring due to poor quality of products sold on its website • Business failure may be more likely to occur due to external causes, which are outside Etsy’s con

Indicative content guidance Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. Knowledge, Application, Analysis, Evaluation – indicative content • Online businesses trade via the internet, using electronic means of payment • Etsy may benefit from trading online because it allows access to sellers and customers from all over the world rather than just in New York where it is based • Costs are likely to be low as there is no need for business premises. Etsy does not even have a warehouse • This may have helped Etsy’s profit margin to grow to 73.4% in the third quarter of 2020 • Online shopping is accessible to far more of the world’s population than traditional shopping (there may not be another outlet for these products) and so Etsy’s growth may be able to continue for years to come • Further growth of e-commerce and the number of internet users may add to this, as well as help grow the number of sellers from the 3,681 reached in 2020 • However, competition from other businesses trading online is high, especially from well-known rivals such as eBay and Amazon • If Etsy’s competitors are able to take a percentage of the market share from Etsy as a result of having global access from the online trading, this could reduce Etsy’s 2020 third quarter revenue from $451.5m. • Low set-up costs and Etsy’s success (an increase from 44,807 buyers in the third quarter of 2019 to 69,649 a year later) could lead to a further increase in competition • Being an online business may attract more competitors globally, however, the size, growth and success of Etsy may enable the business to withstand new entrants to the market through advertising and its ability to differentiate • As an online business, Etsy may suffer from technical breakdowns a

Knowledge 1, Application 2, Analysis 1 Quantitative skills assessed: QS2: Calculate, use and understand percentages and percentage changes QS5: Calculate cost, revenue, profit and break-even Knowledge 1 mark for identifying the formula for calculating fixed costs as a percentage of total cash outflow: • Fixed costs x 100 (1) Total cash outflow Application Up to 2 marks for selecting the correct data from Extract D: • $6 500 + $2 000 (1) x 100 OR $8 500 (1) x 100 $17 500 (1) $17 500 (1) Analysis 1 mark for calculating the fixed costs as a percentage of total cash outflow: • 48.57% (1) NB: If no working is shown, award marks as follows: • If the answer given is 48.57% award 4 marks • If the answer given is 48.57 award 3 marks (4) Using the data in Extract D, analyse two possible problems for DFS from its cash flow forecast from April – July 2021. Mark Quantitative skills assessed: QS9: Interpret, apply and analyse information in written, graphical and numerical forms Knowledge Up to 2 marks for defining cash flow forecast, e.g. • A cash flow forecast shows cash inflows and outflows (1) over a future period of time (1) OR For stating up to two negative implications, e.g. • Declining credit sales/cash inflows (1) • A negative monthly net cash flow is likely to continue (1) Application Up to 2 marks for answers contextualised to DFS, e.g. • Forecast total cash inflows fell from Aus$53 500 in April 2021 to Aus$14 375 in July 2021 (1) • In July 2021, DFS forecasts its net cash flow to be (Aus$2 125) (1) Analysis Up to 2 marks for reasons/causes/consequences for DFS, e.g. • This means that DFS may need to find ways to earn higher revenue throughout the year, perhaps by changing the way membership fees are paid (1) • Therefore, DFS may need to make plans to cover the shortfall if it does not have a sufficient balance from previous months (1) (6) Discuss whether a grant would be a suitable method of finance for DFS. Indicative content

Indicative content guidance Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. Knowledge, Application, Analysis, Evaluation – indicative content • A grant is a sum of money given by a government or other organisation • It does not need to be repaid and no interest is required • This means that DFS could benefit from receiving a grant as it would increase cash inflows • An increase in cash inflows would be especially helpful from June onwards, as net cash flow is expected to reduce considerably (from Aus$16 625 in May to Aus$2 875 in June) once the majority of film society membership has been paid for the year • Increasing the net cash flow (and keeping it positive) may allow DFS to screen more charitable and special events, thus increasing its support of charitable organisations • Without finance from a grant, DFS may struggle to fund as many special events, especially if demand for screenings at the Deckchair cinema falls • However, in order to obtain finance from a grant, DFS would need to prove it qualifies for the finance by meeting the criteria • The necessary documentation required by the Australian Government or other organisations to be successful in being approved to receive a grant may take a considerable amount of time to complete • This may prove problematic as only a few employees are paid by the society and volunteers may not be as willing or as keen to complete lengthy paperwork • Alternative methods of finance may be more suitable for DFS such as an overdraft or bank loan Level Mark Descriptor 0 No rewardable material. Level 1 1–2 Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding – recall based. Weak or no relevant application to business examples. Generic assertions may be presented. Level 2 3–5 Accurate know

Indicative content guidance Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. Knowledge, Application, Analysis, Evaluation – indicative content • Environmental protection legislation is designed to reduce the impact of businesses and protect the environment • It is put in place by a government and can reduce pollution, destruction of wildlife and depletion of resources • The Container Deposit Scheme (CDS) began in 2012 in Darwin, reducing the number of bottles discarded, causing litter and/or contributing to unnecessary landfill • Not only does DFS follow the legislation but it encourages fundraising for local charities through use of recycling bins • This could promote the reputation of DFS and its Deckchair cinema, encouraging further custom at the same time as not increasing costs for the society • The legislation compliments the ethical stance of DFS allowing the society to fulfil its objectives more easily • However, consumers of bottled drinks may want to keep the Aus$0.10 themselves and so the provision of recycling bins may have a limited effect • Although not required by the legislation, the provision of free filtered water is a cost to DFS and it might reduce sales of other bottled drinks made by the society/the refunds to support local charities • As a result, this would reduce revenue made by DFS and/or the positive impact on DFS • There may be additional environmental protection legislation with which DFS needs to comply. It could be difficult to ensure the film society complies effectively as it is mainly run by volunteers Level Mark Descriptor 0 No rewardable material.
